skill.name: conversation-memory
skill.description: Persistent memory systems for LLM conversations including short-term, long-term, and entity-based memory
skill.summary: Build AI assistants that remember users across conversations with tiered memory storage and intelligent retrieval
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Documentation-only skill with no executable code. Static analyzer flagged Markdown backtick formatting as shell commands (false positive) and misidentified description text as cryptographic patterns (false positive). No actual security risks detected. Safe for publication.

content.user_title: Build AI memory systems that remember across conversations
content.value_statement: AI assistants often forget context between sessions, frustrating users. This skill implements tiered memory systems for persistent, intelligent conversation recall.
actual_capabilities: Implement short-term memory for active conversation context
Create long-term memory storage for cross-session recall
Build entity-based memory for tracking people, projects, and topics
Design memory consolidation strategies to prioritize important information
Implement intelligent retrieval to surface relevant memories
Manage memory lifecycle to prevent unbounded growth
limitations: Requires external storage mechanism for persistence beyond session
Memory retrieval accuracy depends on quality of indexing strategy
Does not provide built-in storage backend implementation
User isolation must be implemented separately for multi-user systems

use_cases.title: Personal AI Assistant
use_cases.description: Build an assistant that remembers user preferences, project details, and conversation history across months of interactions
use_cases.target_user: Individual users wanting personalized AI assistance

use_cases.title: Customer Support Bot
use_cases.description: Create support bots that recall customer history, previous issues, and preferences for contextual support
use_cases.target_user: Customer support teams building AI-powered help desks

use_cases.title: Research Assistant
use_cases.description: Develop research companions that track research topics, cited papers, and evolving hypotheses over time
use_cases.target_user: Researchers and academics using AI for literature review

prompt_templates.title: Basic Memory Setup
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a simple conversation memory system that stores key facts about the user and retrieves them in future conversations. Include functions for storing memories and retrieving relevant ones based on the current query.

prompt_templates.title: Tiered Memory Architecture
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a tiered memory system with short-term (current session), medium-term (recent sessions), and long-term (permanent facts) storage. Define criteria for promoting memories between tiers and implement retrieval that queries all relevant tiers.

prompt_templates.title: Entity Memory Network
prompt_templates.prompt: Build an entity-based memory system that tracks relationships between people, projects, and topics. Implement graph-like storage where entities link to related entities, with functions to traverse connections and surface contextual memories.

prompt_templates.title: Memory Consolidation System
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a memory consolidation system that periodically reviews stored memories, merges duplicates, removes outdated information, and strengthens important memories through rehearsal. Include scheduling logic and consolidation criteria.

output_examples.input: User asks to remember their project details
output_examples.output: Memory stored: User is working on 'Project Phoenix' - a React dashboard for analytics. Deadline: March 2026. Team size: 5 developers. Technology stack: React, Node.js, PostgreSQL.

output_examples.input: User returns after 2 weeks and mentions Phoenix
output_examples.output: Retrieved memories: Project Phoenix (analytics dashboard, 5-person team, March deadline). Last discussion covered database schema design. Would you like to continue from the schema discussion or start a new topic?

best_practices: Implement memory lifecycle policies to prevent unbounded growth and system slowdown
Use relevance scoring to prioritize which memories to retrieve for each query
Enforce strict user isolation to prevent memory leakage between different users
anti_patterns: Remember everything without filtering - this overwhelms context and degrades performance
No retrieval logic - storing memories without intelligent retrieval makes them useless
Single memory store - mixing all memory types in one store reduces retrieval accuracy

faq.question: How do I persist memories beyond a single conversation session?
faq.answer: This skill provides the memory system design patterns. You need to implement external storage (database, file system, or vector store) to persist memories between sessions. The skill shows you how to structure and organize that storage.

faq.question: What is the difference between short-term and long-term memory?
faq.answer: Short-term memory holds active conversation context (current session only). Long-term memory stores important facts across sessions. Short-term is fast and complete; long-term is selective and requires retrieval.

faq.question: How do I prevent the memory system from growing too large?
faq.answer: Implement memory lifecycle management: set maximum memory counts per tier, define expiration policies for stale memories, consolidate duplicate memories, and use importance scoring to prune low-value memories.

faq.question: Can this skill work with Claude Code and Codex?
faq.answer: Yes, the memory patterns in this skill are compatible with Claude, Claude Code, and Codex. The memory system design is platform-agnostic and works with any LLM that supports conversation context.

faq.question: How do I ensure memories from one user are not accessible to another?
faq.answer: Implement strict user isolation at the storage layer. Each user should have a separate memory namespace or partition. Never mix user memories in shared storage without user ID scoping on every read and write operation.

faq.question: What is entity memory and when should I use it?
faq.answer: Entity memory tracks facts about specific entities (people, projects, organizations) and their relationships. Use it when you need to maintain structured knowledge about interconnected topics and query by entity rather than by conversation context.
